macro_segments.title: Establishing Kiddushei Kesef from the Father's Sale of His Daughter into Slavery

micro_segments.title: Setting Up the Question: How Do We Know Kiddushei Kesef and the Father's Role
The Case of a Minor Daughter: Father's Power Over Money and Marriage
Extending to the Na'arah: Can the Daughter Keep the Money if She Marries Herself?
The Gemara's Answer Using the Verse on Slave Sales
Kol Shevach Neurim: All Benefits of Youth Go to the Father

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing Three Categories of Father's Rights: Slavery, Labor, Vow Annulment, and Kiddushin

micro_segments.title: Charting Three Distinct Rights: Sale as Slave, Ma'aseh Yadeiha (Labor), Hifrat Nedarim (Vow Annulment), Kiddushei Kesef
Why Can't We Learn Kiddushei Kesef from Hifrat Nedarim?
Why Can't We Learn Kiddushei Kesef from Knas (Fine)? Monetary vs. Penal
Boshet and Pegam: Real Compensation, Not Knas—Why Doesn't the Father Get Kiddushei Kesef?

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing Three Categories of Father's Rights: Slavery, Labor, Vow Annulment, and Kiddushin (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Father's Rights Over Boshet and Pegam: Connecting to the Power to Marry Her Off

macro_segments.title: The Murky Logic of Boshet, Pegam, and Kiddushei Kesef for the Na'arah

micro_segments.title: Rashi's Explanation: The Father Lost Money by Not Marrying Her Off Before Damage
Why Doesn't Boshet and Pegam Prove Kiddushei Kesef? The Gemara's Struggle
Tosafot's Resolution: He Can Still Marry Her Off as Na'arah, So He Deserves Boshet
Final Answer: Misavra—Reasoning from Ein Kesef to Father Gets Money Always

macro_segments.title: Comparing Slave Departure from Master to Daughter Departure from Father After Kiddushin

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Challenge: She Doesn't Really Leave Her Father's House After Kiddushin
Father's Continued Control Over Hifrat Nedarim: Shared Authority with the Husband
Resolving the Parallel: Limited Change of Status for Vow Annulment, Not Real Departure
The Transactional Model of Kiddushei Kesef Based on Slavery Analogy

macro_segments.title: Economic Realities and the Shift from Real Money to Symbolic Kiddushei Kesef

micro_segments.title: Historical Context: The Fifty Silver Coins (200 Zuz) and Financial Burden on Daughters
The Irony of Symbolic Kiddushei Kesef: Father Now Bears Dowry Cost Instead
Ketubah as Balance: Money from Bride's Family and Groom's Obligations

macro_segments.title: The Double Language of Yatzah Chinam and Ein Kesef: Deriving Multiple Lessons from One Verse

micro_segments.title: Why Does the Verse Say Both 'She Goes Free' and 'Without Money'? The Extra Yud
Rabina's Interpretive Method: Using the Extra Yud to Derive Multiple Teachings
Analogy to Zera Ein Lah: How the Word Ayin Can Teach Multiple Laws
Both Pesumim Needed: Ein Kesef Teaches Two Things—Freedom and Alternative Money Transfer

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing the Verses by Necessity: Why Both the Slave Sale and the Father's Authority Are Required

micro_segments.title: If Only Kesef Were Written: Might Assume Money Belongs to Her, Not the Father
If Only Yatzah Chinam: Might Assume Money Goes to Her Because She Worked to Earn It
Both Verses Show Reciprocity: Father Supports, Daughter Earns—This Extends to Kiddushei Kesef
Kiddushei Kesef Is Unexpected; Therefore Father's Rights Must Be Explicitly Taught

macro_segments.title: Clarifying the Status of the Na'arah: When Can She Marry Herself vs. Father's Authority

micro_segments.title: The Father's Power to Marry a Na'arah Against Her Will
Can a Na'arah Marry Herself? Debate Between Rambam and Other Authorities
Rambam's Ruling: She Requires Prior Paternal Approval, Acts as Father's Shaliach
Alternative Views: Father's Approval After the Fact May Validate Her Self-Marriage

macro_segments.title: The Double Language Principle: Why Both 'Na'arot' and 'Bagrut' Are Mentioned in the Slavery Verse

micro_segments.title: Why Mention Both Na'arot and Bagrut When a Slave Can Only Be Sold as Ketana?
Rabba's Resolution: Two Phrases Teach That Freedom Applies to Both Stages
The Principle of Bazzeh U'Limad Al Zeh: One Phrase Would Apply to the Later Stage Only

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Aylonit: A Woman Who Becomes Bogeret Without Ever Becoming Na'arah

micro_segments.title: Who Is the Aylonit? A Woman Without Puberty Signs Who Reaches Age 20
The Concern: If She Skips Na'arot, Does She Avoid Freedom at All?
The Gemara's Teaching: Ein Kesef Confirms Freedom Even for Aylonit at Bagrut
Mar Bar Rav Ashi's Counter-Question: Isn't This a Kal Va'Chomer?
The Gemara's Response: The Verse Teaches a Distinct Chidush—Selling the Aylonit Is Permitted

macro_segments.title: Why Can't We Learn Kiddushei Kesef from the Father's Sale of a Slave Daughter Alone?

micro_segments.title: The Braita: Introducing the Teaching on Kiddushei Kesef from Ki Yikach
The Initial Kal Va'Chomer: From Amah Ivriyah (Slave) to Woman (Marriage)
The Counterargument: Yevamah as Contrast—She's Acquired by Biah, Not Money
Why Yevamah Isn't a Perfect Comparison: Only Biah Works, Not Shtar or Kesef
Therefore Kesef and Shtar Must Be Derived from the Pasuk About Marriage, Not Slavery

macro_segments.title: The Necessity of Both Verses: Ein Kesef (Slavery) and Ki Yikach (Marriage)

micro_segments.title: If Only Ki Yikach Were Written: Might Assume the Woman Keeps the Money
The Model Without a Father: Abraham and Ephron Suggest a Two-Party Transaction
If Only Ein Kesef Were Written: Ein Kesef Doesn't Specify Who Gives vs. Receives Money
Rashi's Hava Amina: Perhaps She Gives the Money and Says 'Marry Me'
Ki Yikach Clarifies: He Must Be the Taker (Active Party in Kiddushin)

macro_segments.title: The Fundamental Asymmetry: His Status Doesn't Change; Her Status Changes (Mekudeshet)

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's Insight: Even if She Acts, She Becomes Mekudeshet and He Does Not
Alternative Reading: She Says 'I Am Mekudeshet to You' (Status Declaration)
Tosafot's Full Resolution: The Hava Amina Means He Gives Money and She Speaks the Declaration
Not Pshat of Gemara: Tosafot Reframes to Align With Father Receiving Money

Ki Yikach Ish Isha (Husband Takes Woman—Marriage Pasuk)
